questions.question: Explain the principle of locality and describe its two main types. Why is this principle fundamental to the effectiveness of cache memory?
questions.modelAnswer: The principle of locality states that programs tend to access a small portion of memory repeatedly. Temporal locality refers to accessing the same items again soon, while spatial locality involves accessing nearby items. Cache leverages this by storing recently used data (temporal) and fetching adjacent blocks (spatial), reducing access latency.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the write-through and write-back cache update policies. Discuss one advantage and one disadvantage of each approach.
questions.modelAnswer: Write-through updates main memory immediately, ensuring consistency but increasing bus traffic. Write-back delays updates until cache line replacement, reducing traffic but requiring 'dirty bits' to track changes. Write-through is simpler but slower; write-back is faster but complex.

questions.question: Define the term 'cache hit' and 'cache miss'. What is one primary cause of a compulsory miss when a program first begins execution?
questions.modelAnswer: A cache hit occurs when requested data is found in cache; a miss requires fetching from main memory. Compulsory misses happen on first access as the cache is initially empty.
